name: Olympia Games
description: Olympia, located in Elis in the Peloponnese peninsula, was a sacred sanctuary of ancient Greece and the site of the ancient Olympic Games. Dedicated to Zeus, the king of the gods, Olympia held immense religious and cultural significance for all Greeks. The Olympic Games, held every four years, were more than just athletic competitions; they were a celebration of religious piety, physical prowess, and panhellenic unity. A sacred truce, the 'ekecheiria,' was declared throughout Greece during the Games, ensuring safe passage for athletes and spectators. The sanctuary housed temples dedicated to Zeus and Hera, along with numerous statues of gods and athletes. The games included various athletic contests, such as running, wrestling, boxing, chariot racing, and the pentathlon. Victors were crowned with olive wreaths and achieved immortal fame. The site was also used for other religious festivals and ceremonies. The prominence of Olympia gradually declined with the rise of Christianity and the suppression of pagan practices. The Games were officially banned in 393 AD by Emperor Theodosius I, marking the end of an era for this sacred place and the traditions it fostered. The enduring legacy of Olympia lies in its contribution to the spirit of athletic competition and the ideal of peaceful coexistence among diverse communities.
subtitle: Birthplace of the Olympic Spirit

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Games
keyMyths.description: The myth surrounding the founding of the games varies, but one popular version attributes it to Heracles, who established the games in honor of his father, Zeus, after completing his twelve labors. He measured the distance of the stadium with his own feet.

keyMyths.title: Pelops and Hippodamia
keyMyths.description: The story of Pelops winning Hippodamia as his bride through a chariot race, after bribing the charioteer of King Oenomaus, is linked to the founding of the games and the importance of chariot racing at Olympia. Pelops is said to have honored Zeus after his victory.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: Detailed account of Olympia and its history.
sources.text: Detailed account of Olympia and its history.
